messages.content: Is "He is of legal age" a better translation than "He has reached the age of majority" for the japanese sentence "彼は成年に達している”?
Yes, "He is of legal age" is a more natural translation for "彼は成年に達している".
I did not say more natural but better! Which one does really cover what was said in japanese?

Both translations, "He is of legal age" and "He has reached the age of majority", cover what was said in Japanese. However, "He is of legal age" is a more concise and commonly used phrase in English to convey the same meaning. Therefore, it may be considered a better translation in terms of being a clearer and simpler expression of the same idea.
You mean that "to reach" and "to be" have the same meaning?

Well, "to be" and "to have reached" have different grammatical structures, but in the context of the phrase "彼は成年に達している," both "He is of legal age" and "He has reached the age of majority" convey the same meaning. So, both translations express that he is at a legal age in which he has responsibilities and rights given by the law.
They expresses the same idea but "to be" is not what is said in japanese

Yes, you are right that "to be" is not what is literally said in Japanese. The phrase "彼は成年に達している" literally translates to "He has reached the age of majority." However, in this context, both "He is of legal age" and "He has reached the age of majority" can be used interchangeably to convey the same idea. 

So, it would be more accurate to say that "He has reached the age of majority" is a more literal translation, while "He is of legal age" is a more idiomatic and commonly used expression in English to convey the same meaning.

Yes but in some countries when you reach the age of majority you get rights that you did not have before. But when you are of legal age you do not you may already have it for a long time. Thus it does not convey exactly the same meaning.

You are correct in saying that the phrase "of legal age" and "age of majority" do not always convey the same exact meaning in all legal contexts across different countries. 

In general, "of legal age" implies that an individual has reached the age at which the law grants certain rights or privileges, such as the legal ability to vote, obtain a driver's license, get married, or purchase alcohol. On the other hand, "age of majority" typically refers to the age at which a person is considered an adult and is granted full legal rights and responsibilities, such as the right to sign contracts or be called for jury duty. 

So, depending on the specific legal context in question, one translation may be more appropriate or accurate than the other.
Thus is there another way in japanese to say that he is of legal age, meaning that he did not just reach it, but have it for a while.

Yes, there is another way in Japanese to express that someone is of legal age and has had it for a period of time. Instead of using the phrase "成年に達している" (ninen ni tasshiteiru), which strictly means "has reached the age of majority," you can use the phrase "成年である" (ninen de aru) to express that someone is "of legal age" as opposed to just having recently reached it. 

This phrase "成年である" (ninen de aru) can be translated as "is of legal age" or "has attained legal age," which implies that the individual is not just newly of legal age but has been so for some time.
So, why to say that “he is of legal age” is a better translation for "彼は成年に達している" as both languages have specific ways to express the difference between "to reach legal age" or "to be of legal age"?

You are correct in pointing out that both Japanese and English have specific phrases to distinguish between "reaching legal age" and "being of legal age." 

When translating from one language to another, we try to find the most accurate and appropriate way to convey the original meaning in the target language. In the case of "彼は成年に達している," which can be translated as "He has reached the age of majority," the translation "He is of legal age" is also a valid and appropriate translation as it accurately conveys the main idea. 

Both phrases communicate the idea that the person is legally recognized as an adult. While it is true that "reached the age of majority" is more accurate and specific than "of legal age," in some contexts, the difference between the two may be minimal, and in those contexts, both phrases could be used effectively. 

So, in summary, both "He has reached the age of majority" and "He is of legal age" are valid translations of "彼は成年に達している," and the choice depends on the context and intended audience.

Yes but if a japanese uses "成年に達している" instead of “成年である” that certainly means that he had a specific idea in his given context to say it like that and not in the other way. So in the same context while by translating the other way you change the intention of the speaker. That could be consider as if the translator would like to change the meanings of the speaker.

That is a valid point. In some contexts, there may be a specific reason why someone would use the phrase "成年に達している" to convey the idea of being of legal age. It could be to emphasize the process of reaching the age of majority or to contrast the individual's current status with their status as a minor. 

When translating, it is important to consider the context and intended meaning of the original language. If the Japanese speaker was emphasizing the process of reaching legal age, it would be appropriate to use "He has reached the age of majority" as the translation. On the other hand, if they were simply stating that the individual is currently of legal age, it may be more appropriate to use "He is of legal age" as the translation. 

The translator's role is to accurately convey the meaning and intention of the speaker to the target audience, so it is important to consider the nuance of the original language in making translation choices.

I cannot understand why, in any context, the choice of the japanese has its importance, he could ha chosen the other alternative, but he did not. The question is why he did not, and the translator cannot change it for him. Whatever the context that was his choice to opt for this one and not the other. So why another person would have the right to change its choice without knowing why he did not choice the other solution?

You are right that as a translator, it is important to respect the choices made by the original speaker or writer and accurately convey their intended meaning in the target language. 

Different phrases can be used to express the same idea, and it is possible that the original speaker or writer chose a particular phrase for a specific reason. In some cases, the reason for a speaker's choice may be clear based on the context. In other cases, the reason may be more ambiguous or uncertain.

As a translator, it is important to consider the context and available information to ensure the translation accurately reflects the original speaker's or writer's intended meaning. However, it is also important to acknowledge that language is complex and nuanced, and translations may not always capture all of the nuances of the original language. 

In short, as a translator, it is essential to be respectful of the original speaker's or writer's choices while also accurately conveying their intended meaning in the target language.
And if you do not know the context nor the intention of the speaker, then which one is better?

If the context and the intention of the speaker is not clear, it is best to translate the Japanese phrase "彼は成年に達している" in a way that allows for both possibilities.

One possible translation that could convey either option is "He has attained legal adulthood." This translation captures the idea of having reached the age of majority while also allowing for the possibility that the individual is currently of legal age. 

Another possible translation that captures both possibilities is "He is legally an adult." This translation allows for the possibility that the speaker is indicating that the person has only recently reached the age of majority or has been of legal age for some time.

In summary, when the context and intention of the speaker is unclear, it is best to choose a translation that allows for both possibilities and captures the essence of the original phrase.

Because, if I say it in my maternal language (french), when I say "Il a atteint l'âge légal" instead of "il a l'âge légal", I would not be happy that somebody says that I said "Il a l'âge légal"... For me it is not exactly the same thing.

You are correct that "Il a atteint l'âge légal" and "Il a l'âge légal" do not convey exactly the same thing in French. The former emphasizes the milestone of reaching the legal age, while the latter emphasizes the current status of being at legal age.

The same applies to "He has reached the age of majority" and "He is of legal age" in English, where the former emphasizes reaching the legal age milestone and the latter emphasizes the current status of being at legal age. 

Therefore, as a translator, it is important to distinguish between the two phrases and to choose the appropriate one that accurately reflects the original intention of the speaker or writer. If the context and intention are unclear, the translator should provide a translation that captures both possibilities, as I mentioned earlier.
I do not really know if it is the same in english, but I am quite sure that a japanese will not be happy to, as me.

You are correct that a careful translator should be mindful of the original speaker's intent when translating between languages. Even if the difference between "He has reached the age of majority" and "He is of legal age" may seem subtle in English, it could have a different connotation or emphasis in Japanese or other languages. 

If the intention behind using "彼は成年に達している" is specifically to emphasize the process of reaching legal age, then translating it as "He has attained legal adulthood" would be more accurate than "He is of legal age." However, if the intention is just to communicate that the person is of legal age, then "He is of legal age" would be more appropriate. 

As a translator, it is important to be attentive to any subtle differences between languages and to try to understand the original speaker's or writer's intent as much as possible to ensure that the translation reflects their intended meaning as accurately as possible.

Une autre question, la phrase "古くから偏見は中々なくならない" signifie elle que "à notre époque comme dans l'antiquité, il y a toujours des préjugés" ou que "les préjugés de l'antiquité sont toujours existant et les même à notre époque"?

La phrase "古くから偏見は中々なくならない" peut se traduire littéralement en français comme "Les préjugés sont difficiles à éliminer depuis longtemps" ou "Les préjugés ont longtemps été difficiles à éliminer". 

Cette phrase exprime l'idée que les préjugés existent depuis longtemps dans la société, et qu'au fil du temps, ils sont devenus ancrés dans la culture et les mentalités. Elle ne précise pas si ces préjugés sont toujours les mêmes de nos jours que dans l'Antiquité, mais elle indique que les préjugés sont un problème persistant et difficile à résoudre.

Par conséquent, on peut traduire cette phrase comme "Il y a toujours des préjugés, depuis longtemps dans l'histoire jusqu'à nos jours" ou "Les préjugés sont un problème ancien et tenace qui persiste jusqu'à présent".
Et en anglais, que signifie la phrase "Old prejudices just won't go away"?

The phrase "Old prejudices just won't go away" can be translated as "Les vieux préjugés ne disparaissent tout simplement pas" in French. 

In English, this phrase means that long-standing or deep-seated prejudices continue to persist despite efforts to eliminate or overcome them. It implies that the problem of prejudice has persisted for a long time and is not easily resolved.
